The Back Pain Blueprint
02
Welcome
A Message
From Dr. Rudy

If you're reading this, you've probably already seen a doctor. Maybe two. Maybe five. You've had imaging done, been told things look "normal," been handed exercises that didn't work — and you're still in pain. I see this every single day.

Here's what I've learned after treating hundreds of patients at CVC Spine & Sport: back pain almost never comes from where it hurts. The real source is almost always upstream — a tight psoas, a weak glute, a nerve pattern that's been ignored because no one asked the right questions.

Before I was a chiropractor, I was a Coast Guard veteran, a competitive athlete, and a guy who dealt with his own pain and figured out that the healthcare system is built around specialties — and specialties mean specialists who only look in their lane. No one was connecting the dots.

This guide is built around my diagnostic framework: ask broad questions, consider every variable, and use eliminating logic to trace the problem to its actual source. It's not magic. It's systems thinking applied to your body.

Take this seriously. Do the self-assessment. If you need more, we're here.

— Dr. Rudy
Doctor of Chiropractic  ·  CVC Spine & Sport  ·  Colleyville, TX
The Core Belief
Pain is a signal, not a diagnosis. Your body is telling you something is wrong upstream. This guide teaches you to listen to the signal — not just silence it.
"The problem is rarely where the pain is."

03
What This Guide Delivers
Four Promises
To You
Inside this blueprint, you won't find generic stretches or copy-pasted YouTube routines. You'll find a doctor-led diagnostic framework built from years of clinical practice — designed to help you stop treating symptoms and start solving the actual problem.
01
Find the Real Source
We'll walk through the eliminating question framework that helps identify where your pain is actually originating — not just where it hurts.
02
Alleviate, Restore, Rebuild
Every phase of this program is sequenced: calm the pain first, restore mobility second, build lasting strength and resilience third.
03
Know When to Get Help
I'll tell you clearly when this guide is enough and when you need to see someone — and exactly what to tell them when you do.
04
Build Lifelong Resilience
The goal isn't to get you to "pain-free." It's to get you to a body that doesn't break down again. Resilience is the finish line.
CVC Spine & Sport
The Back Pain Blueprint
04
The Dr. Rudy Diagnostic Framework
The 7 Eliminating
Questions
Why This Works
Most specialists look only in their specialty. I look at the whole system — and ask questions designed to eliminate possibilities until the real source becomes clear.
"Start broad. Narrow down. Never assume."
01
When did this start, and what were you doing?
Acute vs. chronic onset tells us whether we're dealing with an injury or a pattern that's been building.
02
Does the pain travel, radiate, or stay in one place?
Radiating pain suggests nerve involvement — which means the source is likely far from where you feel it.
03
What makes it better? What makes it worse?
Positional changes reveal which structures are under load — disc, muscle, facet, or nerve root.
04
How much time do you spend sitting each day?
Prolonged sitting compresses the psoas and loads the lumbar discs — two of the most overlooked contributors to back pain.
05
Do you have any digestive, urinary, or systemic symptoms?
Referred pain from internal organs can mimic musculoskeletal back pain — and gets missed when no one asks.
06
What does your sleep position look like?
Hours of spinal loading during sleep is one of the most undertreated sources of chronic morning stiffness and pain.
07
What does your hip and glute strength look like?
Weak glutes and tight hip flexors shift load to the lumbar spine — the most common root cause I see in clinical practice.
CVC Spine & Sport
The Back Pain Blueprint
05
Patient Behavior Assessment
What You're Doing
Right & Wrong
✓   7 Things You're Doing Right
✓
Seeking help early Early action — even self-guided — makes recovery significantly easier and faster.
✓
Listening to your body Respecting pain signals instead of pushing through sharp or shooting pain.
✓
Prioritizing posture Instinctively modifying how you sit, stand, or sleep to reduce discomfort.
✓
Staying active Walking, stretching, and changing positions instead of complete bed rest.
✓
Using heat/ice correctly Cold in the acute phase; heat for muscle tension and chronic stiffness.
✓
Staying consistent Even light daily movement compounds and leads to meaningful results.
✓
Being patient with the process Understanding that healing takes time and is not linear.
✗   7 Things You're Doing Wrong
✗
Stretching into pain Forcing a stretch when your body is resisting almost always makes it worse.
✗
Cracking your own back Temporary relief, but often creates instability and worsens the underlying problem.
✗
Avoiding all movement Too much rest lets the body stiffen and weaken — slowing recovery significantly.
✗
Focusing only on where it hurts Missing the root cause by treating the location instead of the source.
✗
Returning to exercise too soon Lifting heavy or high-impact training before the tissue is ready often causes re-injury.
✗
Ignoring warning signs Waiting until pain is unbearable before acting dramatically worsens outcomes.
✗
Following generic routines One-size-fits-all YouTube rehab rarely matches your specific body and symptoms.
CVC Spine & Sport
The Back Pain Blueprint
06
Your Recovery Protocol
The 5-Phase
Blueprint
Each phase is sequenced with intention. Don't skip ahead. The goal is not just to reduce pain — it's to build a body that doesn't break down again.
1
Phase
Restore — Calm the System
Days 1–5
Reduce inflammation, calm muscle guarding, and create the right environment for healing. Gentle movement, breathing, and positioning are your tools. No aggressive stretching.
2
Phase
Reactivate — Wake Up the Pattern
Days 6–10
Reawaken underused muscles — especially glutes, deep core, and hip flexors — and begin reconnecting proper movement patterns. This is where most programs skip straight to strength and fail.
3
Phase
Realign — Restore Mechanics
Days 11–17
Restore joint mobility and retrain healthy movement mechanics under load. Begin addressing the upstream contributors — hip mobility, thoracic extension, and lumbar stability.
4
Phase
Reinforce — Build Protection
Days 18–24
Build lasting strength and stability in the muscles that protect your spine. By now you're not just avoiding pain — you're building armor against future injury.
5
Phase
Resilience — Live Without Limits
Days 25–30 & Beyond
Translate your strength into daily life and sport. The goal is a body that moves on purpose — and one you trust completely. This phase never ends.
CVC Spine & Sport

The Back Pain Blueprint
08
Dr. Rudy's Clinical Protocol
What Are You
Feeling?
Not all pain is the same — and the treatment shouldn't be either. Use this decision tree to identify what your body is telling you, then follow the protocol that matches your symptoms.
You Feel
Tension &
Restriction
What it means
Your muscles are tight and guarded. The muscle fibers are contracted and resisting movement — often from overuse, stress, or poor posture.
Dr. Rudy's Protocol
1
Apply Heat — 15–20 min to relax the muscle fibers and increase circulation
2
Active Low-Impact Movement — walking, gentle hip circles, cat-cow to get the muscle moving
3
Massage Gun — 60–90 sec on affected muscle group at low–medium intensity
Do NOT stretch aggressively into it
You Feel
Ache or
Deep Pain
What it means
The joint itself is likely involved — a tendon, ligament, or disc under stress. The tissue is inflamed and needs to calm down before loading.
Dr. Rudy's Protocol
1
Apply Ice — 10–15 min to decrease inflammation in the joint and surrounding tissue
2
Rest the joint — avoid loading or compressing the area. Gentle unloaded movement only
3
Reassess in 48 hrs — if not improving, seek clinical evaluation. Don't push through joint pain
Heat can worsen joint inflammation
You Feel
Isolated Pain
+ Tension
What it means
Both the joint and surrounding muscles are involved. Contrast therapy addresses both simultaneously — a clinical favorite for complex low back presentations.
Dr. Rudy's Protocol
1
Contrast Therapy — alternate 3 min heat / 1 min ice × 3 rounds to stimulate circulation and reduce inflammation
2
Dynamic Movements — gentle pelvic tilts, bird-dogs, and dead bugs to reactivate stabilizers
3
Massage Gun + QL/Psoas Stretch — release the tight structures before loading any movement
Always end on cold (1 min ice)
!
Red Flag — Seek Immediate Care
If you experience pain radiating down the leg below the knee, loss of bowel or bladder control, numbness/tingling in the groin or inner thighs, or pain that does not change with any position — stop this program and see a clinician immediately. These are signs of nerve or spinal cord involvement that require clinical evaluation.
CVC Spine & Sport
The Back Pain Blueprint
09
The Root Cause Framework
Lower Crossed
Syndrome
This is the pattern I see in the majority of my back pain patients. It's not a diagnosis — it's a movement dysfunction. Two muscle groups are too tight, two are too weak, and your spine is paying the price.
Overactive — Need to Stretch
Psoas / Hip Flexors
Chronically shortened from sitting. Pulls the pelvis forward, creating anterior tilt and compressing the lumbar discs.
Quadriceps
Tight quads reinforce anterior pelvic tilt and shift load from the glutes to the lumbar spine during movement.
QL (Quadratus Lumborum)
Deep low back muscle that locks up under chronic stress and compression. One of the most common sources of acute "throwing your back out."
Lumbar Erectors
Over-recruit when the glutes and core aren't doing their job — leading to chronic spasm and fatigue in the low back.
Underactive — Need to Strengthen
Glutes (Max, Med, Min)
The primary hip extensors — and the most commonly inhibited muscle group in people with low back pain. Weak glutes = the lumbar spine absorbs every step.
Deep Core (TVA, Multifidus)
The inner corset of the spine. When these shut down, the spine loses its foundation — and every movement becomes a liability.
Hamstrings
Underused when glutes are inhibited. Strengthening the hamstrings restores proper posterior chain function and takes load off the lumbar spine.
Low Back Stabilizers
Targeted strengthening — not generic back work — restores segmental control and reduces the muscle guarding that causes chronic stiffness.
Stretch These
Psoas  ·  QL  ·  Quads  ·  Lumbar Erectors
Strengthen These
Glutes  ·  Core  ·  Hamstrings  ·  Low Back
CVC Spine & Sport
The Back Pain Blueprint
10
Your Weekly Resilience Protocol
What a Week
Looks Like
Each week follows the same structure. The exercises progress in difficulty as you move through the phases — but the weekly rhythm stays consistent so it becomes automatic.
Monday
Mobility + Core Activation
Cat-Cow  ·  3×10 slow reps
Pelvic Tilts  ·  3×15
Dead Bug  ·  3×8 each side
Psoas Stretch  ·  3×45 sec/side
QL Side Stretch  ·  2×30 sec/side
Focus: Restore baseline mobility. No pain.
Tuesday
Glute + Hamstring Activation
Glute Bridge  ·  4×15
Clamshells  ·  3×15/side
Romanian Deadlift  ·  3×12 bodyweight
Quad Stretch  ·  3×45 sec/side
15 min walk  ·  flat surface, easy pace
Focus: Wake up glutes. Take load off spine.
Wednesday
Active Recovery + Stretch
20 min Walk  ·  easy pace, no incline
Psoas Stretch  ·  3×60 sec/side
Figure-4 Piriformis  ·  3×45 sec/side
Child's Pose  ·  3×60 sec
Massage Gun  ·  QL + glutes, 90 sec each
Focus: Recovery. Let the work from Tue sink in.
Thursday
Core + Low Back Strength
Bird-Dog  ·  4×10/side, hold 3 sec
Plank  ·  3×20–45 sec
Superman Hold  ·  3×10, hold 3 sec
Side Plank  ·  3×20 sec/side
QL Stretch  ·  3×30 sec/side
Focus: Build the spinal corset. Slow and controlled.
CVC Spine & Sport
The Back Pain Blueprint
11
Your Weekly Resilience Protocol — Continued
Friday Through
Sunday
Friday
Full Posterior Chain
Single-Leg Glute Bridge  ·  3×12/side
Hamstring Curl (band)  ·  3×15
Good Mornings  ·  3×12 bodyweight
Psoas + Quad Stretch  ·  3×45 sec/side
Hip Flexor Mobility Flow  ·  5 min
Focus: Connect glutes → hamstrings → spine.
Saturday
Full Body Movement + Stretch
Goblet Squat  ·  3×12 bodyweight or light load
Lateral Band Walk  ·  3×15 steps/side
Hip 90/90 Stretch  ·  3×45 sec/side
Full Spine Mobility Flow  ·  10 min
Massage Gun  ·  full lower body, 60 sec/area
Focus: Move well in all planes. Reinforce the pattern.
Sunday
True Rest + Mindful Recovery
Sleep
Prioritize 7–9 hrs. Your spine heals while you sleep. Sleep position matters — see insert.
Breath Work
5 min diaphragmatic breathing. Activates the deep core and reduces systemic inflammation.
Reflect
Review your tracker. Note what worked. What felt better this week vs. last?
Mon
Mobility
Tue
Glutes
Wed
Recovery
Thu
Core
Fri
Chain
Sat
Full Body
Sun
Rest
CVC Spine & Sport
The Back Pain Blueprint
13
The Science Behind Your Experience
You're Not Broken.
Your Nervous System
Just Learned the Wrong Lesson.

If you've had back pain for months or years, here's something your doctors may never have told you: a significant part of what you're feeling may be your nervous system, not your tissue.

This isn't the same as saying "it's in your head." The pain is absolutely real. But the nervous system is adaptive — and when it lives in a pain state long enough, it begins to anticipate pain before any structure is actually threatened. Your pain threshold drops. Your system becomes hypervigilant. Bending over triggers a pain response before you've even reached a range that would stress the tissue.

The good news: this is reversible. The nervous system can be reeducated. Movement is the medicine. Understanding this is one of the most powerful tools in your recovery.

The Clinical Term
Central Sensitization
Amplified neural signaling that lowers pain threshold and creates hypersensitivity to normal stimuli.
Research Shows
Most chronic LBP patients have no specific structural cause — pain persists through central sensitization.
— Kim & Lee, Medicina, 2023
The Biopsychosocial Model of Pain
Biological
Tissue injury, inflammation, nerve compression, disc pathology — the parts that imaging can see. Important, but often not the whole story.
Psychological
Fear of movement, pain catastrophizing, anxiety and depression. Research shows these predict chronic pain development as strongly as tissue damage.
Social
Work demands, relationships, socioeconomic stress, cultural beliefs about pain — all shown to directly influence pain intensity and recovery outcomes.
Kinesiophobia — Fear of Movement
An excessive, irrational fear of physical movement caused by a belief that movement will cause re-injury. Research finds kinesiophobia is present in up to 67% of chronic low back pain patients — and predicts disability far better than pain intensity alone.
Vlaeyen & Linton, 2000; PMC 2023 review
Pain Catastrophizing
The tendency to ruminate on pain, magnify its threat, and feel helpless. Catastrophizing is present in up to 65% of chronic LBP patients and is one of the strongest predictors of disability and failure to recover.
Sullivan et al., 2001; Picavet et al., 2002
What the Research Shows
Pain Neuroscience Education works.
A 2023 systematic review of 15 RCTs confirmed that teaching patients how pain works significantly reduces pain, disability, and fear.
Lepri et al., 2023
The Outcome Data
A 2023 meta-analysis of 9 RCTs with over 1,000 chronic LBP patients found significant short-term reductions in pain intensity and catastrophizing — with effects that carried over at follow-up.
Kim & Lee, Medicina, 2023
The Umbrella Review
Multiple meta-analyses confirm a moderate-to-large effect on pain reduction (SMD −0.53 to −0.85), especially combined with exercise and manual therapy.
Frontiers in Neuroscience, 2023
"
The moment a patient understands that their pain threshold has simply been recalibrated by a nervous system trying to protect them — not by damage that can't be healed — everything changes. Fear drops. Movement becomes possible. That shift is often worth more than any adjustment I can give them.
— Dr. Rudy  ·  CVC Spine & Sport
